# Transcrição: 1-10 - Thesis Statement

- **Fonte:** 1-10 - Thesis Statement
- **Duração:** 15:52
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay, lesson one, we're going to be talking about a story's thesis statement. In order to come up with the thesis statement, which is possibly the most important step in all of the storytelling process, we have to talk about what is a story. Ultimately, and fundamentally, we have to understand what is a story and just as important, that is not a story so that we can craft something that's going to mean something to other people. The problem with storytelling today is that most people don't understand what constitutes a story. Most often, instead of telling a story, people report on their lives, which is to say, they have something happen over the course of the day and they tell you what happened over the course of the day, usually in chronological order. And they think of that as a story. Something happened to me and now I have a story to tell. That is not a story that is simply reporting on your day, relaying events of the day to someone else in a way that is uncrafty, unartful, and probably not very entertaining. The real tragedy in storytelling, the reason why so much bad storytelling exists in the world is because 99.999% of all stories that you've ever told in your entire life have been told to people who like and love you. So as you tell a story poorly, you receive positive feedback from the people who love you because they smile and nod, which makes sense. They love you. They want to hear what happened during your day. They want to hear the events of your vacation. They want to know what happened while you were in school, but it doesn't mean you're telling a good story, a well-crafted story. So we move through our lives telling bad stories and receiving positive feedback for it. And so we never understand when we're telling something good and when we're telling something bad. The only real way that you can tell whether you're a good storyteller is you have to stand in front of people who don't like you, an objective audience. You have to stand in front of them and tell a story and receive a response that indicates you have done a good job. And most people don't have the opportunity to do that. And the results is bad storytelling. So what is a story? Fundamentally, a story is about change over time. I used to be one kind of person and then some stuff happened and now I'm a new kind of person. That is what a story really is. That change comes in one of two forms. It's transformation, which means I used to be one type of person and then some stuff happened that's the bulk of the story. And as a result, I am a new type of person or the more common version of change, which is realization. I used to think one thing about myself, my spouse, my children, trees, grass, the sun. It can be anything. I used to think one way about something and then some stuff happened. Again, that's the bulk of the story.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

And in the end, I think differently. That is essentially what a story is. And that's all the great stories of the world. Whether you're watching a movie, reading a book, watching a television show, whether you're sitting in a theater in Broadway or listening to a storyteller on a stage perform, ultimately, all of those stories are about a singular protagonist or a collection of protagonists who move through time and change over the course of that time. That's what you're looking for. You're looking for moments of change in your life. I call them five second moments because I believe that they happen instantaneously. There's a great deal of things that lead up to that change. A lot has to happen. And again, that's the bulk of your story. But when you change your mind, when you see yourself in a new light, when you suddenly decide to be a better or a worse version of yourself, when you transform in some way, it's instantaneous. A lot leads up to it, but that singular moment is what we're looking for. That is a five second moment, and that is what constitutes an actual story. So as you come into this process, you need to have a story to work with. And so ultimately, you're looking for a story that has that moment of change. You may already have a story in your mind. You probably do. Most people do. You need to ask yourself, is it really a story or is it just stuff that took place over time? Sometimes we have really extraordinary things happen to us. Really fantastic, exciting, unbelievable moments in our lives, but they don't constitute any change. We don't change as human beings over the course of these amazing moments. And so ultimately, these stories tend not to be stories. They tend to be a report of an admittedly fantastical, amazing, unbelievable moment, but they don't stay with the listener. They don't stay with your audience. They don't make an impact on your audience. They tend to be really fun stories when you're sitting out for drinks or walking the golf course. There are stories that will make people laugh and gasp, but they're not the kind of stories that make people reconsider who they are, their place in the world. They're not the kind of stories that make people feel deeply connected to you. So what we're doing is looking for stories that have a moment of change. That's what you need to find in order to begin crafting your first story. If you have a bunch of choices of stories and you're trying to choose which one to begin with, let me offer you a couple of suggestions. First, choose the simplest story, especially if this is the first story you're ever really going to craft in a serious way. Smaller, simpler stories are simpler to craft and easier to take my strategies and apply them. Simpler stories mean they take place over a shorter period of time. They have fewer characters and they have fewer locations.

## [06:02 - 09:03]

Choose those stories to start with. Those are always the stories I prefer to tell anyway. Those are the easier stories for audiences to listen to, absorb, and understand. So you're looking for a story that contains change and ultimately, if you can, find one of those simpler stories. Once you find it, it's time to make your thesis statement, to construct the thesis statement, which will essentially be our map to the end of a story from beginning to end. I never craft a story ever without having a thesis statement in place first. Once I have a thesis statement, I know exactly what has to go on the story and what needs to be taken out. A thesis statement, which you've already heard me say several times, goes something like this. I used to be this and then some stuff happened and now I'm this. Right? I used to think this and then some stuff happened and now I think this. The easiest type of thesis statement to think about is a romantic comedy. If you've ever seen the movie when Harry met Sally, the thesis statement is pretty simple. I used to hate Sally and then some stuff happened and now I love Sally. And for Sally, it's the same one. I used to hate Harry, then some stuff happened and now I love Harry. That is the thesis statement for that story for both of those protagonists. If you think about the original Star Wars films, those are films actually about religion if you look at them carefully. Luke Skywalker. I used to be a boy on a desert planet hoping to someday fly a spaceship and defeat the empire. And then some stuff happened and in that moment when I found myself in a spaceship ready to defeat the empire, I turned off all of my technology and used something called the force religion to defeat the empire instead. That is a boy who did not have religion and then some stuff happened and he found religion and he used that religion to defeat evil. It's a story about religion. Han Solo in that movie, by the way, also has a thesis statement. I used to be selfish and then some stuff happened and in the end I became selfless. Now you don't need to defeat an empire. You don't need to fall in love to have a thesis statement. Small changes are actually some of the best changes that can ever happen in a story because people believe in small changes. Our lives are filled with small changes. Very few of us defeat empires. We don't fall in love that often. But small moments of change, those are the ones that people connect to because those are the ones that people understand. The story that I'm going to be telling or at least putting together for you is about my kids. About 10 years ago, I was in a McDonald's play place with Clara and Charlie.

## [09:03 - 12:03]

Charlie was about three or four at the time and he had climbed into the play place into these tubes. He had climbed up this tall tower, crossed over this tube about 12 feet in the air, crossed over this net section of the tube and was all the way on the other side when it was time for him to come back. And so as he was crawling back through the tubes, he came to that sort of net mesh section and he got stuck. He started to get worried. Even though he had already crossed it once, for some reason in his four-year-old mind, crossing it back was untenable and he began to cry. And I couldn't get up there. The tower was tight and narrow. It said, if you're an adult, don't climb in this thing. There was no way I could cross that tube 12 feet off the floor. It was just small and high and it was not going to support my weight. Charlie was stuck in that tube. Now I had Clara with me, but Clara wanted nothing to do with those tubes. She was afraid of the heights. She was a little claustrophobic. She did not want to climb into those tubes and she was my only option. And so after some cajoling and some begging and some pleading, Clara finally went up that tower, crossed over to the tube and came to that mesh section. So Charlie's on one side and Clara's on the other and I'm below them just watching this happen. And Clara reaches her arm across the mesh because she's afraid too and says, Charlie, take my hand and he won't. And I'm getting angry. I say, Charlie, take her hand. And then Clara says something that I will never forget. She says, Charlie, whisper the name of the person you love most and that will give you the courage you need. Can you believe that? Clara was six years old and somehow she came up with those words. Charlie, whisper the name of the person you love most and that will give you the courage. That's my five second moment. Everything that happens after that is utterly irrelevant to the story. That is the moment I'm trying to speak to in my story. I'm trying to tell you that there was a moment of time when I listened to my daughter speak words that I did not put into her mind and into her heart, words she found on her own, words a six year old found, wisdom she found that helped her brother get out of those tubes and out of trouble. For me, it was the moment I realized my kids are no longer entirely tethered to me. That there's a moment in life when every single thing your kid knows came directly from you. And then over the course of time, they start to learn other things from teachers and friends and television. And pretty soon you figure out that they know a lot of stuff that you didn't teach them. But that was the day I discovered Clara figured out stuff on her own.

## [12:03 - 15:06]

No one had ever given her that strategy before. She came up with it herself, whisper the name of the person you love most and that will give you courage. It was the moment I realized my kids are going to be okay. That if I get hit by a bus tomorrow, they have the wisdom and the knowledge and all of that stuff that you want them to have to get through life. That's what I'm trying to say in that story. And so my thesis statement is going to go something like this. I once was a father who thought his kids needed him in order to survive that they relied on me in order to get through the day in one piece. And then some stuff happened, the bulk of my story. And I realized in that five second moment that the kids are going to be okay, that they're going to make it even if I am not there to hold their hands anymore, that they're going to figure it out on their own. That's my thesis statement. Once I have that thesis statement in place, I know exactly what has to go in my story and I know what doesn't belong in my story. Everything in my story has to serve the thesis statement, which in this case is I'm worried about my kids. I think they depend upon me. I don't think they can survive without me. I need to talk about the play place. I need to talk about Charlie getting stuck. I need to relay the events that take place as she climbs up the tubes, Clara, and then I need to give you that five second moment. Anything else I want to put in that story does not belong. I take it out. A thesis statement is a roadmap to your story. It tells you where to begin and it tells you where to end. Never start crafting a story without a thesis statement. If you start crafting a story, not knowing where you're going or where you should begin, you're probably just reporting on life. You're probably just relaying events over the course of time and you don't want to do that. So your job now is to find your thesis statement. My suggestion, start with your five second moment. Start with the moment that you're trying to share with your audience. For me, what I really wanted to do was tell my audience one day, 10 years ago, my daughter said something so extraordinary. It changed the way I view her and it changed the way I see myself as a parent. That's what I'm trying to say. The story is the context that will get you to that moment so it will have the same impact on you that it had on me. So start with your five second moment. What are you trying to say? What is the singular moment in your life that you want to share with other people?

## [15:06 - 15:52]

Once you find it, ask yourself, what is the opposite of that moment? And that will probably be the beginning of your story and that will create your thesis statement. That's your job. Create a thesis statement for the story that you plan on crafting. Create the roadmap that you need so that you will know what goes in a story and what comes out of a story. What belongs and what doesn't belong. Start with your thesis statement. When you have it in place and you believe in it, it's what you're trying to say. Go on to the next video. Good luck. Thank you.
